stories.title: Apartment Units Replacing Vintage Dallas Hotel Rooms
stories.excerpt: The Cabana Motor Hotel along Stemmons Freeway was once a go-to destination in Dallas. The Beatles and Monkees stayed there and Raquel Welch once worked the cocktail lounge. After opening in 1963, the property, which has had numerous owners over the years, including the county jail system, is now being transformed into a 175-unit apartment complex by Sycamore Development. After Sycamore purchased it in 2023, they completely gutted it and began converting the rooms. The Dallas Business Journal reports the firm wanted to preserve and incorporate historical or original parts of the hotel, including bringing back the building’s lighting system and putting back a portion of the original signage. Sycamore also restored much of the original pool and pool deck. The property is listed in the National Register of Historic Places as an example of mid-century modern design. The conversion represents a $70 million investment. Pre-leasing is expected to begin this month. Connect Texas Multifamily in Dallas on August 13 brings together the owners, developers, brokers, investors, and lenders shaping the future of Texas multifamily for a day of market intelligence, networking, and actionable insights. Join 300+ decision-makers and be part of the conversations driving the Texas multifamily market. Register today: www.ConnectTXMF2026.com. The post Apartment Units Replacing Vintage Dallas Hotel Rooms appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Sycamore Development converting 175-unit Dallas hotel to apartments; $70M investment.
Historic Cabana Motor Hotel on Stemmons Freeway; pre-leasing begins this month.
Preservation of mid-century modern design; adaptive reuse signals supply tightness in core Dallas.
stories.summary: Sycamore Development is converting the historic Cabana Motor Hotel in Dallas into a 175-unit apartment complex with a $70 million investment. The property, a National Register-listed mid-century modern structure from 1963, will retain original architectural elements including lighting and signage. Pre-leasing begins immediately, signaling market confidence in Dallas core submarkets.
stories.impact: Adaptive reuse of landmark hospitality assets into multifamily reflects both supply scarcity in established Dallas submarkets and capital availability for repositioning deals. Preservation requirements and higher conversion costs ($400K per unit) are offset by scarce, entitled land and near-term lease-up demand along Stemmons corridor.

stories.title: $100M Luxury Apartment Complex Slated Near Highland Park
stories.excerpt: Cover2 Capital is building a 20-story, $100 million apartment tower close to the border of Highland Park. Dubbed The Savannah, the development will include a rooftop pool and only 100 units. Units are expected to average 1,800 square feet and the Dallas Business Journal reports rents will be on the high end of the scale. The Savannah will have a mix of one-, two- and three-bedroom units, including three penthouse apartments. Construction of The Savannah is scheduled to begin in late 2027 and will take about 30 months to complete. Cover2 Capital targets multifamily and mixed-use development opportunities across Dallas-Fort Worth, focusing on sites supported by strong demographics, durable demand drivers and long-term neighborhood value creation, according to its website. The firm has developed more than 11,000 multifamily units and overseen roughly $2 billion in development value. The post $100M Luxury Apartment Complex Slated Near Highland Park appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Cover2 Capital building 100-unit luxury tower; $100M; near Highland Park, Dallas.
1,800-sq-ft average units; rents at high end of market; construction late 2027.
30-month timeline signals long development horizon in competitive luxury submarkets.
stories.summary: Cover2 Capital is planning The Savannah, a 20-story, $100 million luxury apartment tower with 100 units near Highland Park in Dallas. Average unit size of 1,800 square feet with premium rents and three penthouses. Construction is slated to begin in late 2027 with a 30-month build schedule.
stories.impact: Extended timeline (start 2027, deliver 2030) exposes Cover2 to Dallas luxury market saturation risk—three years out, near-Highland Park submarkets will have absorbed significant recent supply. Boutique positioning and above-average density (1-BR to 3-BR mix) suggest developer betting on steady demographic strength, but long hold-backs and high carrying costs increase sensitivity to rate environment and rental absorption.

stories.title: Los Angeles Matcha Brand Archives of Us Signs Retail Lease in East Village
stories.excerpt: A Los Angeles-based matcha cafe chain has signed a retail deal for its first New York City location in Manhattan’s East Village. Archives of Us has leased 2,400 square feet at Highpoint Property Group’s 84 Second Avenue, where it will act as a cafe and retail presence for the matcha brand, according to landlord broker Meridian Capital Group. Meridian did not disclose the length of the lease, but the asking rent for the space was $125 per square foot. “This was a rare opportunity to secure a fully fitted space on a prime stretch of Second Avenue,” Meridian’s Noam Aziz, who represented the landlord alongside Carson Shahrabani, said in a statement. “Archives of Us has built one of the most distinctive matcha concepts in the country, and the East Village is an ideal setting for its New York debut.” Milly Cheung and Jordan Cohen of Lee & Associates NYC negotiated on behalf of the tenant but did not immediately respond to a request for comment. The space between East Fourth and Fifth streets was previously leased to tapas restaurant Nai, a concept by chef Ruben Rodriguez that closed in November 2025. The space came fully furnished and built out for a restaurant from the previous tenant, according to Meridian. Archives of Us was started by Nick Kim in April 2025 in Los Angeles. Shahrabani commented that the brand could emerge to be “the La Colombe of matcha in New York City.” Mark Hallum can be reached at mhallum@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Archives of Us matcha café signs first NYC retail lease; East Village Manhattan.
2,400 sq ft at 84 Second Avenue; $125/sq-ft asking rent; lifestyle tenant mix.
stories.summary: Archives of Us, a Los Angeles matcha brand, has leased 2,400 square feet at Highpoint Property Group's 84 Second Avenue in Manhattan's East Village for its first New York location, with asking rent at $125 per square foot. The deal signals retailer confidence in the submarket.
stories.impact: Minimal relevance to multifamily development. Retail lease success in East Village reflects neighborhood vitality and foot traffic, which could support mixed-use development feasibility, but the article contains no supply, demand, or capital signals affecting apartment underwriting or market timing.

stories.title: Ben Brown, Brookfield’s Real Estate Chief, On REITs, Housing, Office and Hospitality
stories.excerpt: Ben Brown is the first to admit it: “I drink my own Kool-Aid.” Brown is co-president and head of real estate in the Americas for Brookfield — and, if you were going to drink a company’s Kool-Aid, you couldn’t do much better. “We’ve built this business for this exact environment,” Brown told Commercial Observer at the firm’s offices at 225 Liberty Street late last month. Indeed, Brookfield’s numbers can only be described as staggering: $258 billion in real estate assets under management; 79,000 apartments; another 74,000 student housing beds; 165 million square feet of logistics space; 189 million square feet of office; 107 million square feet of retail (and 2 million square feet of entertainment space); 43,000 hotel keys…. One could go on. At times of market choppiness and uncertainty, a company of such scale might be tempted to sit back and let events simply shake out as they may, but Brookfield has been anything but passive in the last year and a half. In fact, it’s executed about $9 billion in credit originations, $21.5 billion in equity dispositions, $32.3 billion in acquisitions — oh, and it’s also raised $13.2 billion in new capital. At the center of all this real estate activity is the cheerful, clear-eyed Brown. The father of two girls who recently moved to Brooklyn’s Cobble Hill, Brown grew up in South Florida, the son of a mutual fund adviser who, as a second career, did a little development on the side. Brown went to Northeastern University, and stayed in Boston after graduation to work for a private equity fund. “Honestly, coming to Brookfield was dumb luck,” Brown said. “I sent my resume into a job posting and interviewed with a bunch of people over a couple of days.” Now, he’s in his 17th year at the firm. He sat down with Commercial Observer to describe Brookfield’s recent real estate spree. This was a few days before word got out that Brookfield was expected to buy a 10 percent stake in the $3.5 billion, 6.2 million-square-foot office campus in Manha
bullets: Brookfield's Ben Brown oversees $258B real estate AUM; 79,000 apartments; 74,000 student beds.
Executed $9B credit originations, $21.5B equity dispositions, $32.3B investments in 18 months.
Stated strategy: active deployment in 'exact environment'; scale and capital provide countercyclical advantage.
stories.summary: Brookfield's Ben Brown, co-president and head of real estate in the Americas, oversees $258 billion in real estate assets under management including 79,000 apartments and 74,000 student housing beds. Over 18 months, Brookfield has executed $9 billion in credit originations, $21.5 billion in equity dispositions, and $32.3 billion in investments. Brown indicates the firm is actively deploying capital and using scale to capture market opportunities.
stories.impact: Brookfield's aggressive capital recycling and stated confidence in current environment signals institutional capital remains available for off-market acquisitions and stabilized asset trades, supporting exit liquidity for TCR and peer developers. However, $21.5B in equity dispositions indicate mega-cap players are selectively divesting—watch for secondary market pressure on average-quality Class B assets and repricing of risk.

stories.title: 320-Unit Gilbert Apartment Community Trades to Camden
stories.excerpt: CBRE arranged the sale of The Tyler, a 320-unit multifamily community in Gilbert, Arizona. CBRE’s Asher Gunter, Matt Pesch and Austin Groen represented the seller, StreetLights Residential. Camden Property Trust acquired the property. The Tyler offers studio, one-, two- and three-bedroom floor plans. Community amenities include a pool with cabanas, spa and fireplace, a greenhouse-style coworking space and conference room, a demonstration kitchen, an outdoor entertainment paseo with grilling stations and media lounges, a fitness center, electric vehicle charging stations, bike storage, and a pet wash station. The property is located within Agritopia’s Epicenter district, which offers walkable access to more than 20 local retailers and restaurants, as well as community gathering spaces and events. Camden owns and operates 176 properties totaling approximately 60,000 apartment homes across the United States. StreetLights Residential, founded in 2011 and headquartered in Dallas, is a developer of urban multifamily and mixed-use communities. The company has been recognized as one of the largest multifamily developers in the United States. The post 320-Unit Gilbert Apartment Community Trades to Camden appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Camden Property Trust acquires The Tyler; 320-unit community in Gilbert, Arizona.
StreetLights Residential as seller; property in Agritopia Epicenter; full amenity package.
Institutional consolidation of suburban Phoenix supply by large REITs; market maturation signal.
stories.summary: CBRE facilitated the sale of The Tyler, a 320-unit multifamily community in Gilbert, Arizona, from StreetLights Residential to Camden Property Trust. The property features studio to three-bedroom units, premium amenities including coworking, EV charging, and pet wash, and is located in Agritopia's Epicenter walkable district.
stories.impact: Camden's acquisition of Gilbert trophy assets reflects confidence in Phoenix suburban supply absorption and rental fundamentals. However, repeated institutional REIT consolidation of newer supply signals near-term stabilization and potential market cooling—new entrants to Phoenix should focus on infill/urban submarkets (Tempe, Scottsdale) rather than maturing suburban product in Gilbert, where absorption will slow as inventory ages into secondary markets.

stories.title: Ice Cream Shop Scoop N Scootery to Open at 162 Bleecker Street
stories.excerpt: We all scream for ice cream, and come this fall there will be another option for New Yorkers to grab themselves a sweet frozen treat. Or better yet, have it delivered to their door. Scoop N Scootery, an ice cream delivery franchise, will open its first New York City location in September at Acram Group’s 162 Bleecker Street in Manhattan’s Greenwich Village. The ice cream company signed a 15-year, 1,325-square-foot lease for the property’s ground-floor retail space, according to landlord broker Lee & Associates NYC. Asking rent for the space was $175 per square foot. Mark Kapnick and Jordan Cohen from Lee represented the landlord, while Lucas Kooyman from Alvarez & Marsal Property Solutions represented the tenant. Kooyman did not immediately respond to Commercial Observer’s request for comment. “New York City is a milestone market for any growing retail brand, and there’s no better place to make that debut than Bleecker Street,” Kapnick said in a statement. “Scoop N Scootery wanted a location that immediately establishes its presence in one of the country’s most recognized retail corridors. With exceptional foot traffic, an outstanding mix of food-and-beverage operators and very little available space, Bleecker Street checked every box.” Founded over 10 years ago, Scoop N Scootery started with two guys — Austin Crittenden and Sean Sally — and an ice cream truck. After a year and a half of working out of a food truck — and literally delivering ice cream by scooter — Crittenden and Sally opened their first brick-and-mortar location in Arlington, Va. Now, the franchise has locations in Massachusetts, Florida, Texas, New Jersey and North Carolina. 162 Bleecker Street is an 11-story, commercial building between Sullivan and Thompson streets. It’s unclear whether Scoop N Scootery will replace current retail tenant Li-Lac Chocolates in its new spot. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Scoop N Scootery ice cream franchise signs 1,325-sq-ft lease; 162 Bleecker, Greenwich Village.
15-year lease; $175/sq-ft asking rent; September 2024 opening; delivery-focused model.
stories.summary: Scoop N Scootery, an ice cream delivery franchise, has signed a 15-year ground-floor lease for 1,325 square feet at 162 Bleecker Street in Greenwich Village, New York, with asking rent at $175 per square foot. The concept includes delivery service and will open in September.
stories.impact: Minimal relevance to multifamily development. Retail lease signals neighborhood retail vitality but does not indicate new demand drivers, supply constraints, or capital availability affecting apartment development feasibility or underwriting in Manhattan.

stories.title: Younger Partners Sells 86K-SF Prosper Retail Center
stories.excerpt: Younger Partners Investments (YPI) sold Shops at Prosper Trail, an 86,698-square-foot retail center 35 miles north of downtown Dallas in Prosper, Texas, to Phillips Edison & Company. Built in 2016, Shops at Prosper Trail is 100% occupied. The center is anchored by a Kroger Marketplace. The property benefits from limited immediate grocery competition, with the nearest major competitors ranging from five to eight miles away. An HEB grocery store is currently under construction less than five miles from the center. “This grocery-anchored open-air shopping center has been a wonderful investment for us,” said YPI’s Micah Ashford, “We brought it to full occupancy and exceeded our investors’ expectations with the sale. We anticipate rent growth remaining strong in North Texas as we continue to grow our retail platform.” JLL Capital Market’s Sales and Advisory team of Adam Howells and Erin Myer represented YPI. Connect Texas Multifamily in Dallas on August 13 brings together the owners, developers, brokers, investors, and lenders shaping the future of Texas multifamily for a day of market intelligence, networking, and actionable insights. Join 300+ decision-makers and be part of the conversations driving the Texas multifamily market. Register today: www.ConnectTXMF2026.com. The post Younger Partners Sells 86K-SF Prosper Retail Center appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Younger Partners Investments sells Shops at Prosper Trail to Phillips Edison; 86,698 sq ft.
100% occupied; Kroger anchor; built 2016; 35 miles north of Dallas; limited grocery competition.
HEB opening nearby within five miles; competitive grocery pressure emerging in Prosper submarket.
stories.summary: Younger Partners Investments sold Shops at Prosper Trail, an 86,698-square-foot grocery-anchored retail center 35 miles north of Dallas, to Phillips Edison & Company. The center is 100% occupied with a Kroger Marketplace anchor and benefits from limited nearby grocery competition, though HEB is constructing a location less than five miles away.
stories.impact: Minimal direct relevance to multifamily, but signals macro North Texas growth in exurban submarkets (Prosper) and increasing retail competition. For multifamily developers considering lease-up assumptions in DFW suburbs, rising retail density and HEB entry suggest continued demand for workforce/service-sector workers and support for Class B/C product, though pricing and amenity expectations will rise with retail maturity.

stories.title: S3 Capital Provides $24M Construction Loan for Philly Apartments
stories.excerpt: Ampere Capital Group has landed $24 million of construction financing to develop a mixed-use multifamily project in Philadelphia, Commercial Observer has learned. S3 Capital supplied the loan for the developer’s six-story, 109-unit property building with 14,500 square feet of retail space at 1640 North Hancock Street in Philadelphia’s Fishtown neighborhood. “Fishtown has become one of Philadelphia’s most sought-after neighborhoods, with a great mix of retail, restaurants, and easy access to Center City,” Steven Jemal, managing director of origination at S3 Capital, said in a statement. “The market has worked through a significant amount of new supply over the past few years, but that pipeline is starting to come down. As supply and demand move back into balance, we think the neighborhood is well positioned for the long term.” BayBridge Real Estate Capital negotiated the debt with a team consisting of Matteo Yepes, Jay Miller, AJ Felberbaum, Noah Rothman and Spencer Miller. Located 2.5 miles northeast from Philadelphia’s Center City area, the 122,804-square-foot development will comprise studios, one- and two-bedroom apartments. HDO Architecture is the architect for the project, which is slated for completion in late 2027. Ampere Capital Group did not immediately return a request for comment. Andrew Coen can be reached at acoen@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: S3 Capital provides $24M construction loan; Ampere Capital Group developer; Philadelphia Fishtown.
109-unit, six-story mixed-use project; 14,500 sq ft retail; 1640 North Hancock Street.
Lender notes supply pipeline cooling; supply-demand rebalance supports long-term neighborhood outlook.
stories.summary: S3 Capital has provided $24 million in construction financing to Ampere Capital Group for a six-story, 109-unit mixed-use development with 14,500 square feet of retail at 1640 North Hancock Street in Philadelphia's Fishtown neighborhood. The lender noted that while Fishtown has absorbed significant new supply in recent years, the pipeline is declining and supply-demand dynamics are rebalancing.
stories.impact: S3 Capital's thesis—that Fishtown supply pipeline cooling supports risk-adjusted returns—signals potential window for TCR and peers in Philadelphia core markets before late-2026 absorption normalizes and cap rates re-compress. Developers should prioritize Fishtown entitlements in 2025 for 2027–2028 delivery to capture favorable financing terms before spread widening. Construction cost and labor data in context of $24M for 109 units (~$220K per unit in City) should inform DCF baseline.

stories.title: JLL Secures $332M to Refinance 76-Story Chicago Luxury MF Tower
stories.excerpt: JLL has secured a $275 million refinancing and a $57 million mezzanine financing for NEMA Chicago, a luxury multifamily tower and the city’s tallest all-rental residence. JLL represented the borrower, Crescent Heights, in facilitating the five-year, fixed-rate loan through New York Life Insurance Company. JLL also arranged the mezzanine loan through PGIM’s real estate business. JLL Capital Market’s Debt Advisory team was led by Senior Managing Director Danny Kaufman, Director Medina Spiodic and Analyst Youngsoo Yang. Located at 1210 South Indiana Ave. along Grant Park, NEMA Chicago stands 76 stories and 893 feet tall. Completed in 2019, the property was designed by architect Rafael Viñoly with interiors by David Rockwell. “NEMA Chicago exemplifies the flight to quality we’re seeing in urban multifamily, where properties offering true differentiation through design, amenities and service continue to command premium performance,” said Kaufman. “As Chicago’s tallest all-rental residence with Rafael Viñoly’s architectural vision and Crescent Heights’ hospitality-driven approach, NEMA has established itself as an instant landmark that redefines luxury apartment living in the city.” Join the region’s leading multifamily owners, developers, investors, lenders, and brokers at Connect Texas Multifamily on August 13 in Dallas for a focused discussion on the market forces redefining Texas real estate. Be in the room with the decision-makers driving deals and growth across Texas. Register today: www.ConnectTXMF2026.com. The post JLL Secures $332M to Refinance 76-Story Chicago Luxury MF Tower appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: JLL arranges $275M fixed-rate refi + $57M mezzanine for NEMA Chicago; New York Life lender.
Crescent Heights borrower; 76-story, 893-foot luxury tower on Grant Park; completed 2019.
Refinance signals institutional appetite for high-quality urban multifamily; 'flight to quality' narrative confirmed.
stories.summary: JLL has secured $275 million in fixed-rate refinancing and $57 million in mezzanine financing for NEMA Chicago, a 76-story luxury multifamily tower completed in 2019 and Chicago's tallest all-rental residence, on behalf of borrower Crescent Heights. The five-year fixed loan was placed through New York Life Insurance Company and the mezzanine through PGIM's real estate business.
stories.impact: NEMA's $332M refi stack (achieved at fixed rates in current environment) reinforces institutional REIT and insurance capital preference for trophy urban assets with architectural differentiation and hospitality service—a positive signal for anchor projects in Chicago, but negative for stabilized Class B/C suburban product competing for capital. TCR should prioritize design-driven urban/mixed-use positioning and hospitality-grade service standards to access favorable financing; standard apartment models will face cap rate pressure and rising equity hurdle rates.

stories.title: Housing Notes: Developers are in on the joke: Apologize later
stories.excerpt: I have long had a fascination with the grit it takes to be a Manhattan developer. After all, I taught market analysis to a class of approximately 150 graduate students in Columbia’s Master of Science in Real Estate Development (MSRED) program for 8 years. My students have ended up all over the U.S. and hopefully they learned not to accept “no” as an answer when they’re out in the world. That’s the dividing line since developers tend to ignore the word “no” and keep pushing, when non-developers like me would cower in fear and self-doubt. It helps to look at […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Commentary on Manhattan developer mindset; Harry Macklowe midnight demolitions cited; MetroLoft conversion at former Pfizer HQ.
Nathan Berman's office-to-residential conversion suffered partial collapse; development risk profile elevated.
Generalization: developers ignore 'no,' cycle through boom-bust-restart without accountability.
stories.summary: A commentary on Manhattan developer culture, noting that developers typically push past regulatory and market obstacles and often restart projects after setbacks. The piece references Harry Macklowe's controversial demolition practices and notes that Nathan Berman's MetroLoft is converting the former Pfizer Headquarters into residential but recently experienced a partial collapse during construction.
stories.impact: MetroLoft's Pfizer conversion collapse is a critical red flag for adaptive reuse and office-to-residential conversion risk in Manhattan. Developer reputation and construction execution matter significantly; collateral damage to the conversion narrative if Berman's project fails could chill financing and leasing velocity across NYC office-to-residential pipeline. TCR should independently verify construction methodology and structural feasibility for any office conversions in competition.

stories.title: New York Enacting Nation’s First Statewide Data Center Moratorium
stories.excerpt: Commercial real estate’s booming data center industry is suddenly not in an Empire State of mind. New York Gov. Kathy Hochul signed an executive order Tuesday morning enacting the nation’s first statewide moratorium on construction of the large-scale data center facilities that have been powering the country’s increased usage of AI. The move, which includes a one-year ban on new data centers with at least 50 megawatts, also directs state regulators to create new standards for addressing environmental, energy and water-use impacts. “As data center development threatens to hike up utility bills, deplete our natural resources, and create uncertainty for New Yorkers, it’s my responsibility to take action and lead,” Hochul said in a statement. Hochul’s order comes five weeks after the New York State Legislature passed a bill that would also impose a one-year data center moratorium in an attempt to create more guardrails. Maine lawmakers attempted a similar data center moratorium this past spring before the legislation was vetoed by Gov. Janet Mills. An announcement from Hochul on Tuesday said that the increase in demand for data center development in New York, driven largely by AI, has led to an influx of proposals that could require “massive amounts of energy and water to run and cool thousands of computer servers.” Earlier this year, Hochul also ordered the state’s Department of Public Services (DPS) to begin a program requiring data centers to either pay more for their energy or supply their own, as well as to develop a Generic Environmental Impact Statement (GEIS) to ensure new data centers are being held to “consistent standards.” Once those standards are finalized, the moratorium will be lifted, allowing new projects to proceed as long as they follow state, zoning and local approvals, according to Hochul’s announcement. However, experts from global law firm HSF Kramer said the moratorium is simply the governor’s way o
bullets: New York Gov. Kathy Hochul signs one-year statewide data center moratorium; ≥50 MW threshold.
Executive order directs regulators to set new environmental, energy, water-use standards.
Moratorium aims to prevent utility cost spikes, resource depletion, and regulatory uncertainty.
stories.summary: New York Governor Kathy Hochul has signed an executive order enacting the nation's first statewide moratorium on construction of large-scale data center facilities with at least 50 megawatts. The move comes after the state legislature passed a similar one-year moratorium bill and directs regulators to establish new environmental and utility impact standards.
stories.impact: Limited direct impact on multifamily. However, data center moratoria could redirect capital from tech infrastructure into alternative real estate investments (potentially multifamily) in NYC and surrounding markets. Indirectly, a data center ban reduces clustering of AI/tech tenants in underutilized office space, slowing office-to-residential conversion urgency and capital recycling—a headwind for TCR conversion plays in Manhattan core. Monitor second-order effects on tech employment migration away from NYC.

stories.title: Buyer scores $2M judgment against Mandarin Oriental Boca developer
stories.excerpt: The legal troubles at Penn-Florida’s Mandarin Oriental Boca Raton continue to mount after the developer lost a $2 million judgment.  A Palm Beach County Circuit Court judge sided with Douglas and Debra Jacobs after they filed a lawsuit against Penn-Florida affiliate Via Mizner Owner III, which alleged the developer failed to complete their condo in time, the South Florida Business Journal reported.  The developer announced plans for the 248-unit hotel-condo Mandarin Oriental in 2015, with an initial opening target of 2017, the outlet reported. The complaint alleged that the Jacobses signed a contract to buy a condo in the development […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Penn-Florida affiliate Via Mizner Owner III loses $2M judgment; Mandarin Oriental Boca Raton.
Buyer Jacobs' condo undelivered past Dec. 31, 2025 deadline; 248-unit hotel-condo project.
Thirteen lawsuits filed against Via Mizner; only one judgment; developer faces serial contract breach exposure.
stories.summary: A Palm Beach County Circuit Court judge ruled in favor of buyers Douglas and Debra Jacobs against Penn-Florida affiliate Via Mizner Owner III, awarding a $2 million judgment for failing to complete their condo by the December 31, 2025 deadline. The Mandarin Oriental Boca Raton project, announced in 2015 with a 2017 target, has faced delays. Thirteen buyers have filed lawsuits against Via Mizner, though only one has resulted in a judgment so far.
stories.impact: Mandarin Oriental Boca delays and judgment exposure signal significant execution and financing risk in South Florida luxury hotel-condo segment. For TCR: condo-hybrid models face extended pre-sales-to-delivery cycles, buyer deposit holdback risk, and reputational damage that constrains future capital raising. Avoid hybrid structures unless balance sheet can absorb multi-year carry and buyer litigation costs; pure rental remains lower-risk in soft luxury markets.

stories.title: Chicago proposes $287M TIF raid for Related Midwest’s megaproject The 78
stories.excerpt: Chicago officials are set to throw $287 million of property tax revenue into the megaproject that includes Major League Soccer team the Chicago Fire’s new stadium in the South Loop.  The proposal for the project known as the 78 is being considered by the Chicago City Council Finance Committee. The shift in funding is expected to partially reorganize a tax increment financing subsidy for the project’s developer, Related Midwest, according to Crain’s.  The revised agreement would add $287 million from the Canal/Congress TIF district to the TIF district that the 78 sits in, meaning the project’s developers would not have […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Chicago proposes $287M TIF redirect to Related Midwest's The 78 megaproject; South Loop.
Expanded from original $85M over three years; includes Chicago Fire MLS stadium.
Alderman Conway warns Canal/Congress TIF raid threatens Union Station funding; nine-year horizon.
stories.summary: Chicago city officials are proposing to redirect $287 million in property tax revenue from the Canal/Congress TIF district to support infrastructure for The 78, a mixed-use megaproject in the South Loop that includes the Chicago Fire's new stadium. The revised agreement significantly expands the original $85 million transfer over three years and could consume most of the Canal/Congress TIF revenue for nine years.
stories.impact: TIF reallocation signals Chicago's commitment to South Loop revitalization (positive for The 78 and nearby mixed-use development), but diverts resources from Union Station infrastructure and Canal/Congress area. TCR should assess if any land holdings or pipeline projects benefit from South Loop TIF acceleration; conversely, avoid Canal/Congress-adjacent development that depends on TIF support—funding now redirected and may not return for decade. The 78's scale and public subsidy increase risk of construction cost inflation and timeline pressure affecting nearby land and labor markets.

stories.title: Shvo gets fraud claims tossed in Mandarin Oriental suit
stories.excerpt: Developer Michael Shvo is almost off the hook at the Mandarin Oriental.  A judge in New York Supreme Court last week dismissed claims brought against Shvo and his companies by two condo owners at his Mandarin Oriental Residences, who had alleged fraud and shoddy construction.  The judge’s dismissal likely takes some pressure off Shvo, who has been dealing with numerous lawsuits and disputes in the past year.  “We are pleased that the Court dismissed the overwhelming majority of plaintiffs’ claims, including all claims against Michael Shvo personally,” Shvo attorney Morris Missry said in a statement. “The decision confirms our long-held […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Judge dismisses fraud claims against Michael Shvo in Mandarin Oriental condo suit; Manhattan.
Condo owners (Goodmans) alleged fraud, shoddy construction, disclosure failures on $6.1M one-bedroom.
Shvo-Serdar Bilgili partnership dissolved after fund misuse allegations (2020); settlement undisclosed.
stories.summary: A New York Supreme Court judge has dismissed fraud and shoddy construction claims brought by two condo owners, John and Diane Goodman, against developer Michael Shvo and his companies at the Mandarin Oriental Residences (685 Fifth Avenue). The Goodmans purchased a one-bedroom for $6.1 million but alleged construction defects and that Shvo failed to disclose prior fund misuse allegations and arbitration disputes involving his former partner Serdar Bilgili.
stories.impact: Dismissal reduces Shvo's immediate litigation exposure but does not erase reputational damage or construction quality concerns at trophy properties. For TCR: Shvo's history of partner disputes and construction defect litigation suggests execution risk in ultra-luxury conversions; if competing for similar Fifth Avenue/Gucci building-type projects, emphasize pre-construction quality assurance and third-party inspections. Developer disputes with equity partners (Bilgili) signal governance/transparency red flags in sponsor-side underwriting.

stories.title: Judge tosses suit over Two Roads’ Four Seasons Bahamas. Not so fast, plaintiff says
stories.excerpt: A judge tossed a lawsuit accusing Two Roads Development of sidelining a partner from a roughly $300 million Four Seasons-branded condo project in the Bahamas, but the fight isn’t over.  The partner that sued, Soho Development, led by Delray Beach resident and attorney Roger Stein, says the judge addressed the wrong complaint and not the latest filing. Soho Development last summer sued Two Roads, a South Florida luxury development firm, alleging it excluded Soho from the development, while continuing to use project contracts and confidential information provided by Soho with no compensation to the firm, according to the complaint filed […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Judge tosses Soho Development suit vs. Two Roads Development; Four Seasons Bahamas condo project.
Soho alleges Two Roads sidelined partner; ~$300M luxury project; 67-unit Ocean Club Residences.
Soho claims breach of 2021 NDA; Two Roads counters 'unabashed money grab'; Access Industries partner.
stories.summary: A judge has dismissed a lawsuit filed by Soho Development (led by Roger Stein) against Two Roads Development over a roughly $300 million Four Seasons-branded condo project in the Bahamas. Soho alleged Two Roads excluded it from the 67-unit Ocean Club Residences development while misusing confidential information. Two Roads and Access Industries (Len Blavatnik partner) are developing the project; construction expected to complete in 2028.
stories.impact: Dismissal reduces legal risk but ongoing partnership disputes and NDA breaches in luxury offshore condo development signal capital and operational fragility in that segment. For TCR: pure rental development avoids condo sponsor complexity, buyer holdback risk, and extended pre-sales cycles; if entering international or ultra-luxury condo markets, secure waterproof IP and equity governance agreements and consider JV structures with established local operators to mitigate partner conflict risk.

stories.title: Blake Poston jumps from the gridiron to the high-rise with plans for a $100M Uptown Dallas apartment project
stories.excerpt: A former SMU football player is developing a $100 million, 20-story high-rise just north of Uptown Dallas, around a mile from where he used to drop into coverage on Saturdays.  Blake Poston, who was a part of the Southern Methodist University Mustangs football team for three years and went to Highland Park High School, is also the founder of Cover2 Capital. Poston told the Dallas Morning News that Cover2 is aiming to begin construction on an Uptown high-rise at 5220 McKinney Avenue late next year. The building is dubbed The Savannah, and Cover2 is still in design and city development […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Blake Poston (Cover2 Capital, SMU alum) developing The Savannah; 100 units, $100M; Uptown Dallas.
20-story high-rise; 5220 McKinney Avenue; construction start late 2025; 1,800-sq-ft average units.
Cover2 also developing Wheelhouse (297 units, mixed-use NRH) and Greerhouse (garden, Anna); pipeline growth.
stories.summary: Blake Poston, founder of Cover2 Capital and former SMU football player, is developing The Savannah, a $100 million, 20-story luxury high-rise with 100 units at 5220 McKinney Avenue in Uptown Dallas. Average unit size is 1,800 square feet with three penthouses. Construction is targeted to begin in late 2025. Cover2 also has the 297-unit Wheelhouse mixed-use development in North Richland Hills and the planned Greerhouse garden-style community in Anna in the pipeline.
stories.impact: Poston's emerging developer brand and pipeline (The Savannah, Wheelhouse, Greerhouse totaling ~400+ units) reflect confidence in Dallas Metro growth, but portfolio concentration in Uptown luxury and suburban garden products suggests execution risk for a young firm across multiple market segments. Late-2025 start on The Savannah will deliver 2028–2029, overlapping with high new supply in Uptown (from Articles 1, 2)—market absorption risk is real. TCR should monitor Wheelhouse lease-up metrics to gauge Poston's operational capability before considering site competition or partnership.
